test_text.title: Looking for a Rental House in the Town

audioscript.text: Section 1, you will hear an estate agent talking to a customer who wants to rent a house.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 8.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: How can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Hello, I'm interested in renting a house somewhere in the town.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Uh. Could I have your name please?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, it's Steven Godfrey.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And tell me how many bedrooms you're looking for.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, we'd need four, because I'm going to share the house with three friends.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: The number of bedrooms needed is four, so 4 has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 8.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: How can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Hello, I'm interested in renting a house somewhere in the town.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Right, uh, could I have your name please?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, it's Steven Godfrey.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And tell me how many bedrooms you're looking for.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, we'd need four, because I'm going to share the house with 3 friends.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: OK, there are several of that size on our books.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: They mostly belong to families who are working abroad at the moment.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: What about the location?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It'd be nice to be central.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Uh. That might be difficult, as most houses of that size are in the suburbs.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Still, there are a few.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: What's your upper limit for the rent?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: We'd like something around £500 a month, but we could go up to £600 if we have to.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: But we can't go beyond that.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Do you know how long you want to rent the house for?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: The minimum let is six months as you probably realize.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: We're at college here for two years, and we don't wanna have to move during that time if we can avoid it.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Right, and how soon do you want to move in?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: All our lets start on the 1st of the month.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, as soon as possible really, so that means September 1st.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: OK, let me have a look at what we've got.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Uh. We have photographs of all the houses on our books, so you can get an idea of what they're like.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: There's this one in Oakington Avenue, at £550 a month, combined living room and dining room with a separate kitchen.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It doesn't have a garage, though you can park in the road.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: We'd prefer to have one if possible.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Right, then have a look at this house in Mead Street.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It's got a very large living room and kitchen, bathroom, cloakroom.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: How much is it?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That one's 580 it's very well furnished and equipped.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It also has plenty of space for parking, and it's available for a minimum of a year.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, and there's a big garden.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I don't think we could cope with that to be honest.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: We'll be too busy to look after it.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Then there's this older house in Hamilton Road.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Living room, kitchen, diner, and it has a study.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Uh. 550 a month.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That looks rather nice, but whereabouts in Hamilton road?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Towards the western end.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, that'll be very noisy.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I know the area.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, it's pretty lively, but some people like it though.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, what about this house in Devon Close?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That looks lovely.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: There's a big demand for houses in that area, so prices tend to be quite high, but this one hasn't been decorated for a few years, which has kept the rent down a bit.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It's got a living room, dining room, and small kitchen, and it's 595 a month.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I think it would suit you from what you've said.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Hmm. It sounds fine.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 9 and 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 9 and 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Why is that part of town so popular?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, there's a big scheme to improve the district, and it'll soon have the best facilities for miles around.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: What sort of thing?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: There's a big sports center under construction, which will be very impressive when it's finished.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: In fact, the swimming pool already opened, ahead of schedule, and it's attracting a lot of people.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: what about cinemas? Are there any in the area?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: The only one closed down last year, and it's now in the process of being converted into a film museum.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: The local people are trying to get a new cinema added to the scheme.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I think I heard something about a plan to replace the existing concert hall with a larger one.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, that's due to start next year.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, it sounds an interesting area to live in.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Hmm. Could I go and see the house please?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, of course.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Which TWO facilities in the district of Devon Close are open to the public at the moment?

test_text.title: The National Arts Centre: A Hub of Arts and Entertainment

audioscript.text: Section 2, you will hear a man talking on the radio about a national arts center.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, and welcome to Focus on the Arts.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: I'm your host, Dave Green, and this is your very own local radio program.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: Every Friday evening, we put the spotlight on different arts and culture facilities.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: And look at the shows and events that are on offer in the coming week, and today the focus is on the National Arts Center.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: Now, if you don't already know it yourself, I'm sure you've all heard of it.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: It's famous throughout the world as one of the major venues for classical music.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: But did you know that it's actually much more than just a place to hear concerts?
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: The center itself is a huge complex that caters for a great range of arts.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: Under a single roof, it houses concert rooms, theaters, cinemas, art galleries, and a wonderful public library, as well as service facilities including 3 restaurants and a bookshop.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: So, at any one time, the choice of entertainment there is simply enormous.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: So, how did they manage to build such a big arts complex right in the heart of the city?
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: Well, the area was completely destroyed by bombs during the war in 1940.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: So the opportunity was taken to create a cultural center that would be what they called: 'the City's Gift to the Nation'.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: Of course, it took a while for such a big project to get started, but it was planned in the 60s, built in the 70s, and eventually opened to the public in 1983.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: Ever since then, it has proved to be a great success.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: It's not privately owned, like many arts centers, but is still in public hands - it's run by the City Council.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: Both our National Symphony Orchestra and National Theatre Company were involved in the planning of the project.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: And they're now based there, giving regular performances every week.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: And as the center is open 363 days of the year, there are plenty of performances to choose from.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the broadcast, you have some time to look at question 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer question 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, to give you some idea of what's on, and to help you choose from the many possibilities, we've made a selection of the star attractions.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: If you're interested in classical music, then we recommend you go along to the National on either Monday or Tuesday evening at 7:30 for a spectacular production of The Magic Flute, probably the most popular of all Mozart's operas.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: It's in the Garden Hall, and tickets start at only £8, but you'll have to be early if you want to get them that cheap.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: And remember, it's only on for those two evenings.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: For those more interested in the cinema, you might like to see the new Canadian film, which is showing on Wednesday evening at 8 pm in Cinema 2.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: And that's called Three Lives.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: It's had fantastic reviews, and tickets cost just £4.50, which is a reduction on the usual price of £5.50.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: So, it's really good value, especially for such a great movie.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: But you can see the center's main attraction at the weekend, because on Saturday and Sunday, 11:00 am to 10:00 pm, they're showing a wonderful new exhibition that hasn't been seen anywhere else in Europe yet.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: It's a collection of Chinese art called 'Faces of China'.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: That's in Gallery 1, and it has some really fascinating paintings and sculptures by leading artists from all over China.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: And the good news is that it's completely free, so don't miss it.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: So why not go along to the National Arts Center next week for one or all of these great events?
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: And you can always pick up a program and check out all the other performances and exhibitions on offer or coming soon on almost every day of the year.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: Next week, we'll be looking at the new Museum of Science.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE GREEN

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: DAVE GREEN

utterances.text: And that's called Three Lives.
utterances.speaker: DAVE GREEN

utterances.text: It's had fantastic reviews, and tickets cost just £4.50, which is a reduction on the usual price of £5.50.
utterances.speaker: DAVE GREEN

utterances.text: So, it's really good value, especially for such a great movie.
utterances.speaker: DAVE GREEN

utterances.text: But you can see the center's main attraction at the weekend, because on Saturday and Sunday, 11:00 am to 10:00 pm, they're showing a wonderful new exhibition that hasn't been seen anywhere else in Europe yet.
utterances.speaker: DAVE GREEN

utterances.text: It's a collection of Chinese art called 'Faces of China'.
utterances.speaker: DAVE GREEN

utterances.text: That's in Gallery 1, and it has some really fascinating paintings and sculptures by leading artists from all over China.
utterances.speaker: DAVE GREEN

utterances.text: And the good news is that it's completely free, so don't miss it.
utterances.speaker: DAVE GREEN

utterances.text: So why not go along to the National Arts Center next week for one or all of these great events?
utterances.speaker: DAVE GREEN

utterances.text: And you can always pick up a program and check out all the other performances and exhibitions on offer or coming soon on almost every day of the year.
utterances.speaker: DAVE GREEN

utterances.text: Next week, we'll be looking at the new Museum of Science.
utterances.speaker: DAVE GREEN

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

desc.constraint: NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS AND/OR A NUMBER
NO MORE THAN THREE WORDS AND/OR A NUMBER

answer_key.correct_answer: classical music | concerts | classical concerts | music concerts

test_text.title: Paul's South American Experience and University Module Choices

audioscript.text: Section 3, you will hear a university tutor and a new student called Paul, discussing Paul's work experience and Latin American Studies course.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: I've been reading your personal statement, Paul.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: First, let's talk about your work experience in South America.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: What took you there?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Was it to gain more fluency in Spanish?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, as I'm combining Spanish with Latin American studies, my main idea was to find out more about the way people lived there.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: My spoken Spanish was already pretty good, in fact.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Hmm. So you weren't too worried about language barriers?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: No.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: In fact, I ended up teaching English there, although that wasn't my original choice of work.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: I see.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: How did you find out about all this?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I found an agency that runs all kinds of voluntary projects in South America.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: What kind of work?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, there were several possibilities.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: You mean construction? Engineering work?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, getting involved in building projects was an option.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Then there was tourism.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Taking tourists for walks around the volcanoes, which I actually chose to do, and then there was work with local farmers.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Hmm, but you didn't continue with that project, why not?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Hmm, because I never really knew whether I'd be needed or not.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: I'd thought it might be difficult physically, but I was certainly fit enough.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: No, I wanted to do something that had more of a proper structure to it, I suppose.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: I get demotivated otherwise.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: What do you think you learned from your experience?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It must have been a great opportunity to examine community life.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, but it was difficult at first to be accepted by the locals.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: It was a very remote village, and some of them were reluctant to speak to me.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Although they were always interested in my clothes, and how much I had to pay for them.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Well, that's understandable.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, but things soon improved.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: What struck me was that when people became more comfortable with me and less suspicious, we really connected with each other in a meaningful way.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: You made good friends?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, with two of the families in particular.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Good.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: What about management?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Did you have a project manager?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, and he gave me lots of advice and guidance.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: And was he good at managing too?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That wasn't his strong point.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: I think he was often more interested in the academic side of things than filing reports.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: He was a bit of a dreamer.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Uh. And did you have a contract?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I had to stay for a minimum of 3 months.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: My parents were surprised when I asked to stay longer, 6 months in the end.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: I was so happy there.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: And did anything on the administration side of things surprise you?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: What was the food and lodging like?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Hmm. Simple.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: But there was plenty to eat, and I only paid $7 a day for that, which was amazing, really.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: And they gave me all the equipment I needed, even a laptop.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: You didn't expect that then?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: No.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Well, I'll look forward to hearing more.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: But now let's look at these modules.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: You'll need to start thinking about which ones you'll definitely want to study.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: The first one here is Gender Studies in Latin America.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Hmm. It looks at how gender analysis is reconfiguring civil society in Latin America.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Women are increasingly occupying positions in government, and in other elected leadership positions in Latin America.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I think you'd find it interesting.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: If it was to do with people in the villages rather than those in the public sphere, I would.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: OK. What about second language acquisition?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Do you think I'd find that useful?
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Well, you've had some practical experience in the field, I think it would be.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I hadn't thought about that.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: I'll put that down as a definite then.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: OK, what about indigenous women's lives?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That sounds appropriate.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I thought so too, but I looked at last year's exam questions, and that changed my mind.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Uh. Don't judge the value of the course on that.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Maybe talk to some other students first, and we can talk about it again later.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: OK?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, and lastly, will you sign up for Portuguese lessons?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: My Spanish is good, so would I find that module easy?
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Hmm. Not necessarily.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Some people find that Spanish interferes with learning Portuguese, getting the accent right too.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: It's quite different in a lot of ways.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, I'd much sooner do something else then.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: All right, now what we need to do is...
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Paul decided to get work experience in South America because he wanted
options: to learn about Latin American life.

items.title: What project work did Paul originally intend to get involved in?
Why did Paul change from one project to another?
options: His first job was not well organised.
He found doing the routine work very boring.
The work was too physically demanding.

items.title: In the village community, he learnt how important it was to
What does Paul say about his project manager?
options: He let Paul do most of the work.
He was very supportive of Paul.

items.title: Paul was surprised to be given
options: so little money to live on.

text: What does Paul decide about each of the following modules?
textReadable: What does Paul decide about each of the following modules?

audioscript.text: Section 4, you will hear a lecture about success in business systems.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning everyone.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the last few lectures, I've been dealing with business finance, but now I'm going to move on to business systems.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And in today's lecture, I'm going to talk about what can go wrong when businesses try to copy their own best practices.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Once a business has successfully introduced a new process, managing a branch bank say, or selling a new product, the parent organization naturally wants to repeat that success.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And capture it if possible on a bigger scale.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The goal then is to utilize existing knowledge, and not to generate new knowledge.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's a less glamorous activity than pure innovation, but it actually happens more often as a matter of fact.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However surprisingly getting things right the second time is not necessarily any simpler than it was the first time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, there's been a lot of research into how companies can repeat their previous successes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And it certainly hasn't been confined to the United States.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It seems that most large industries are trying to repeat their own successes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And manage the knowledge they've acquired.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But even so, it has been shown that the overwhelming majority of attempts fail.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A host of studies confirm this, covering a wide range of business settings, branch banks, retail stores, real estate agencies, factories, call centers, to name but a few.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So why do so few managers get things right the second or third time?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Let's consider one reason for failure.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Placing too much trust in the people who are running the successful operation, the experts, shall we say?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Managers who want to apply existing knowledge typically start off by going to an expert, uh, such as the person who designed and is running a successful department store.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And picking their brains.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now this approach can be used if you want to gain a rough understanding of a particular system, or understand smaller isolated problems.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The trouble is, even the expert doesn't fully grasp the whole thing, because when it comes to complex systems, the individual components of the process are interwoven with one another.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The expert never has complete access to the necessary information, and the situation's complicated even further by the fact that experts are usually not aware of their own ignorance.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The ignorance can take various forms, uh, for instance, a lot of details of the system are invisible to managers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some may be difficult to describe, learned on the job, and well known by workers perhaps, but impossible to describe in a way that's helpful.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And there are some things that people know or do that they're not even aware of.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, let's consider two types of mistake that can occur when a manager actually starts to set up a duplicate system to replicate a successful process.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Firstly, perhaps he forgets that he was just trying to copy another process, and starts trying to improve on it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Another mistake is trying to use the best parts of various different systems in the hope of creating the perfect combination.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Unfortunately, attempts like these usually turn out to be misguided and lead to problems.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Why?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Uh. Well for various reasons.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Perhaps there weren't really any advantages after all, because the information wasn't accurate, or perhaps the business settings weren't really comparable.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: More typically, the advantages are real enough, but there are also disadvantages that have been overlooked.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Uh. For example.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The modifications might compromise safety in some way.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So what's the solution?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, I don't intend to suggest that it's easy to get things right the second time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's not, but the underlying problem has more to do with attitudes than the actual difficulty of the task, and there are ways of getting it right.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These involve adjusting attitudes first of all, being more realistic and cautious really.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Secondly, they involve exerting strict controls on the organizational and operational systems, and this in turn means copying the original as closely as possible, not merely duplicating the physical characteristics of the factory, but also duplicating the skills that the original employees had.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Reliance on a template like this offers the huge advantage of built-in consistency.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: It seems that most large industries are trying to repeat their own successes.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And manage the knowledge they've acquired.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But even so, it has been shown that the overwhelming majority of attempts fail.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A host of studies confirm this, covering a wide range of business settings, branch banks, retail stores, real estate agencies, factories, call centers, to name but a few.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So why do so few managers get things right the second or third time?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Let's consider one reason for failure.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Placing too much trust in the people who are running the successful operation, the experts, shall we say?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Managers who want to apply existing knowledge typically start off by going to an expert, uh, such as the person who designed and is running a successful department store.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And picking their brains.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now this approach can be used if you want to gain a rough understanding of a particular system, or understand smaller isolated problems.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The trouble is, even the expert doesn't fully grasp the whole thing, because when it comes to complex systems, the individual components of the process are interwoven with one another.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The expert never has complete access to the necessary information, and the situation's complicated even further by the fact that experts are usually not aware of their own ignorance.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The ignorance can take various forms, uh, for instance, a lot of details of the system are invisible to managers.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Some may be difficult to describe, learned on the job, and well known by workers perhaps, but impossible to describe in a way that's helpful.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And there are some things that people know or do that they're not even aware of.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now, let's consider two types of mistake that can occur when a manager actually starts to set up a duplicate system to replicate a successful process.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Firstly, perhaps he forgets that he was just trying to copy another process, and starts trying to improve on it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Another mistake is trying to use the best parts of various different systems in the hope of creating the perfect combination.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Unfortunately, attempts like these usually turn out to be misguided and lead to problems.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Why?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Uh. Well for various reasons.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Perhaps there weren't really any advantages after all, because the information wasn't accurate, or perhaps the business settings weren't really comparable.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: More typically, the advantages are real enough, but there are also disadvantages that have been overlooked.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Uh. For example.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The modifications might compromise safety in some way.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So what's the solution?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Well, I don't intend to suggest that it's easy to get things right the second time.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's not, but the underlying problem has more to do with attitudes than the actual difficulty of the task, and there are ways of getting it right.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These involve adjusting attitudes first of all, being more realistic and cautious really.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Secondly, they involve exerting strict controls on the organizational and operational systems, and this in turn means copying the original as closely as possible, not merely duplicating the physical characteristics of the factory, but also duplicating the skills that the original employees had.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Reliance on a template like this offers the huge advantage of built-in consistency.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Compared to introducing new business processes, attempts to copy existing processes are
Most research into the repetition of success in business has
options: been done outside the United States.
related to only a few contexts

items.title: What does the speaker say about consulting experts?
options: Too few managers ever do it.
It can be useful in certain circumstances
Experts are sometimes unwilling to give advice.

items.title: An expert's knowledge about a business system may be incomplete because
options: some details are difficult for workers to explain
workers choose not to mention certain details
details are sometimes altered by workers.

items: create an ideal <input> from the best parts of several processes
items.title: Two mistakes - Manager tries to:

items: comparison between the business settings was invalid
disadvantages were overlooked, e.g. effect of changes on <input>
items.title: Cause of problems

body.title: Setting up systems based on an existing process
